speaker: interviewer
text: May 1st, 1996. Survivor, Morris Kesselman, K-E-S-S-E-L-M-A-N. Name at birth, Zidenfeld, Z-I-D-E-N-F-E-L-D. Interviewer, Elise Arden, A-R-D-E-N. City, Hallandale, Florida, United States of America. And the language is English. Today is May 1st, 1996. My name is Elise Arden, A-R-D-E-N. And I am conducting an interview with survivor Morris Kesselman, K-E-S-S-E-L-M-A-N. The location is Hallandale, Florida, United States of America. Could you tell me your name and spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Morris Kesselman, M-O-R-R-I-S K-E-S-S-E-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Zidenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 30, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name of the city and country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city of Lodz was a big city, I think about a half a million people. Half of the people were Jewish, a quarter of a million Jews. Very industrial it was. And they called it the second Manchester because it was a lot of textile factories. A lot of observing Jews, very Orthodox. Jews lived among themselves in certain areas. Jewish kids went in their own schools, although there were public schools, but we did not mix with non-Jewish kids or girls. It was strictly a boys school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home life was a very orthodox home. My father was in textile business. My mother never worked. We lived in an apartment house. It was a tight life. We had a decent life. We were not rich. But family was a big family. And I went to school from 7 o'clock in the morning to 7 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Osher in Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a textile factory, which we made linen towels. And most of the time he was selling it to Warsaw. He was traveling every other week or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any other interests or activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father was involved in medical institutions, helping poor. And also he was also involved in a sort of bank, a free loan bank, which poor people came in and got a loan and they paid back very little. And very seldom did I see my father because an early riser, my father. He went off and I was still sleeping. And when I went to bed he was never home except Saturdays or Friday night, when we went together to temple or we had to get a supper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rachel. In Jewish it was Rachel. She was a homemaker, busy in the house. Home was no machinery or washing machines or dishwashers, so she had her hands full. I mean, it was a full life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a good cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I remember, a very good cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the holidays like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very festive. As I mentioned, my father was very religious, very observant. Well, I had only one sister and one brother. The house wasn't big. Although our intermediate family lived around us, but we were always by ourselves. We got together certain evenings for supper or dinner. There was not too much room to sit down many people. But we went to the temple in the morning. And by the time we came home, we had dinner. And I saw my father lay down. And I tried to get out, play a little ball. But secretly. I shouldn't have done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your brothers and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother's name was Leon. He was taken into the war, into the army. I never know what happened to him. And I'm, to this day, I don't know if he ever survived or he got killed. I had a younger sister. Her name was Sarah. And when I came back to the ghetto from one of the camps, my father told me that she was taken away with a lot of children from the ghetto. And they were all slaughtered.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't be more than eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you went to school until late at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, we-- 8 o'clock-- 8:00 in the morning we had to be in school, which when I go to public school. And that school went on until about 2:00, 2:30. And from there I went what they called cheder in Jewish. It was a religious school. And six days a week we went to cheder. So we came home very late in the evening. But Fridays, because of the Sabbath, we usually were home around 4 o'clock. Or we did not go to the cheder if the days got smaller. When it got dark, we had to be home for the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the holidays? What was Passover like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover was very festive. It's not like here. As I mentioned, we had very small quarters. This gave a chance to refurbish the walls or paint with just white paint, and clean up the whole house. And it took maybe two or three weeks until the house was put in order. And then I remember in the yard came-- they made big kettles of the kashrut over dishes. Here in this country, most people have special dishes they take it out. Not could we afford it to have special dishes or we had a place to put them when you live in a big apartment house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do the dishes then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We kashruted them. And they had big kettles of hot water with stones. They were kashrut, and the special people did it. They would get paid whatever small money. And those dishes were put away for Pesach, the last two days. And we-- my mother did not cook the last day or so. So we ate just sandwiches, whatever was available. And when the holiday started, it was very, very strict and observant. We enjoyed it very much, especially the second night. I remember my father used to say to us to go to lay down because it's going to be a long night. And he could have been sitting around until 1 or 2 o'clock in the morning. And over there we listened and we didn't run away from the table. It was mostly for respect, and we didn't know any other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big Seder. As I say, only a family Seder, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other holidays, like Purim and Rosh Hashana?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosh Hashana was a very not festive holiday. We went to temple. We spent there most of half a day, and we came home. It was observant, reminder of what we went through a whole year. And sort of tried to recollect for the new year, and observed religious. And we ate, and we meet with family. And this was the Rosh Hashana. The Purim festival was very well organized. Even at cheder, we got together and we sang. And we gave each other little gifts. They call it in Jewish [HEBREW]. And it was in the streets people danced with the [NON-ENGLISH], because as I mentioned, the areas where we lived was mostly Jewish. So we had an autonomy to do less or more what we wanted to do. And it was very festive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Hanukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Hanukkah we observe over there the eight days. And we were given one gift, as some families whatever could afford more than the others. And every night we sang. We sent the candles. My mother used to make potato latkes. This was such a ritual. And it was very festive. Here, when we came to this country, and we had show them we had to follow the tradition. And we gave them a gift every night. And I assume it's still going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you went to school with only Jewish children. Did you have any friends? Did you play with any children who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Growing up I did not have a non-Jewish friends. And we did not play with non-Jewish friends. Well, we played with our Jewish friends. We went out, played with soccer balls. The other thing, hand ball we used to throw around. But we didn't have the facilities. Our schoolyard was very small and a lot of windows around. And it was-- we couldn't-- if you break a window, and we lost a ball, we had to pay for the window to get back the ball. So it wasn't good. And then the other thing, we didn't have the time. As I mentioned it to you, we went to cheder and then public school, that there wasn't much time to play outside except Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you interested in Zionism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- first of all I don't know if my father was interested at all. And I was too young to get in in those things. I did not belong to any organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a boy growing up in Poland, what were your goals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, our goals, some of them went to higher education, which was like gymnasium, but it was high school. But you had to pay for it. And I think the majority of the people didn't have the pay to pay for it. I remember my goal was just to go to gymnasium because my father, as I say, was in business. Although we were not rich, but I think we could afford to send the children to the higher education. And probably take on the profession what my father had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally, I didn't, experience it because I didn't mix with any other nationalities. But I had stories. I tell them that we were not very well liked. Poland as a whole was very anti-Semitic. I remember when Pilsudski, which was the president, died. They used to say that he was a grandfather of the Jews. And in that time it really started problems with slaughtering of the kosher cows. They used to stay outside Jewish stores with signs, 'don't buy from Jews.' At 1933, '34, anti-Semitism really came to fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the '30s were you aware of what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the late '30s I was aware of it because Germany took off some of the Jews who were not citizens at all, and they brought them to the Polish borders. Poland wouldn't let them in, and the Germans would push them out. But somehow they did make it into the center of Poland. And I remember they came into the cities, and they were helped by the Jewish community to work at some places, to stay. Or they brought them home mostly Friday night for supper. So I did-- I was aware, before the war, that what's going on with the Jews coming into Poland from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever make any plans to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar Mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The reason why I did not have a bar mitzvah, I was-- at the age of 13 I was already in Germany. When the war broke out I was 12 years old. Tradition by Jews are, is that the boy when he come of the age of 13 has a bar mitzvah. But I never had a bar mitzvah, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change for the Jewish people in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the war broke out-- our town was not bombed because there were more Germans in the town than Poles. And when the Germans came in in the first few days, they used to catch people to work. I don't think in those days they just grabbing Jews. They grab anybody who they could. Because the town was dug up for tanks, holes, and all of this. This had to be closed up. And food start getting scarce. And big lines were forming in the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Germans come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in about seven or eight days. I would say September 8 or 9 into Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that the Germans were about to come into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We actually sat in the bunkers, in the basement, which is a basement not a bunker. And we heard some shooting. And I remember elderly people saying it can't be that bad. I hope they stop shooting. Let them come in already. And one morning people came down and told us that the military is marching in big parades.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't feel one way or another. I mean, I know it's not going to be the same as it was. But I felt there is a war going on, and how I long can it last? Maybe when it's over things will come back to normalcy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What laws were set up against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every day you could have seen-- the first thing what they did is that there was notes up on the walls that anybody who had a radio had to give it up. Anybody who had automobiles, which were very few, had to be given up. Factories were confiscated. Schools were closed. And every day was a new notice. If-- here is for anybody has gold, or anybody has some-- anything good of value, had to bring it to the Jewish community. Or to like a Judenrat, which was a committee. And there the Germans were there and collecting. Some brought, some didn't bring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was on the committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one particular person, which was the leader of the [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was the oldest of the committee of the Judenrat. The others I did not know by name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father's business affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe two or three weeks, he tried to bring home some stuff into the house, which he did. But we didn't have any money. There was no-- he couldn't get any raw material. And it stopped right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then how did your father make his livelihood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, we had a little money, and he was be able to take home some of the materials. And they were exchanging for food and all this because they brought in right away German money. Nobody had any money. They didn't know what the money is going to be worth. So it was strictly a bartering exchange.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: They burned some of the synagogues. Somebody [INAUDIBLE] a mine and explode. I remember one particular synagogue. It was the largest synagogue, the oldest, and the biggest. They called it the Altshtot, Wolborska. They tried to bomb that building many times. They couldn't break it apart. It's so heavy bricks. And when I left Lodz, I don't know what happened to it. I never went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family able to stay in their home during the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My family stayed in the home until I left Lodz. They were still in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was their registration of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume yes, because they had to be given a new piece of paper with what they called a card, a food card. Oh, sorry, there was registration, what trade you have. And they opened up shops. But I wasn't involved in it. I don't know, my father did whatever he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was arrested by German middle of November, towards the end of November in 1939. I was kept two days in a place what they called [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12 Years old. And then several thousand of us were shipped out to Germany. This was not Jews alone. This was people in general. And I had pretty good luck. I was placed on a farm with several other people. And as a 12 year old I just milked the cows. I took some straw. I had some light work. And life wasn't bad. We had food. I mean nothing spectacular. We had bread. We had soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to say goodbye to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. When I was in the city, you know when they took me into that holding place, my father did not know what happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to write to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from Germany. Later on in the next camp I wrote to them, and I did get an answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like in this camp in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a camp. As I mentioned, I was on a farm. I slept in a barn. As a young boy I really didn't mind. I know there's a war going on. One of those days it's going to come to an end. And we're going to go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to make friends with other workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not friends. I was the youngest among them. I knew who they were, but they didn't know exactly who I was until a later date that they found out that I was a Jew. And this didn't help. And they gave us out, particularly me, to the gestapo. And one morning I was rounded up into a place where I came, and several other people were there. And kept us a couple of days. And we were sent out from there to another camp, to a Jewish camp, to Posen, which was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled by train. It was a regular train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you arrived at the new camp in Posen?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 Years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came in nobody was there yet. This was a stadium, a football stadium. And under the seating there were big rooms. They put up three bunks up with straw, just plain straw. And we slept with each other very tight. But it took a few days until the first transport came in from Lodz ghetto, 1,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They assigned us to kommandos to work. And in those days, we were marched to work by German, volks German. They were Germans from Poland, which they spoke Polish and German. They did not have guns or rifles, but they had night sticks. And we were marched to work. And we did work. Not everybody worked the same type of work. Where I worked we were building a road. And I for one got lucky. And I was working on a small locomotive, switching the railing over, jumping up and down. But the other people were loading the small lorries with dirt the whole day, working twelve hours a day. So fortunately, I didn't have it bad at work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who decided what kind of work you would do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we came to the place of work, they have Germans there, mostly local Germans. And they had the [NON-ENGLISH]. And they needed 10 people here and 10 people there. And I guess they picked them by strength or by height. I don't know. Because you didn't have to have a trade or profession to be working anyplace. It was all hard labor, strictly manual labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked 12 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in the morning when we left the camp, we got some soup-- coffee, which they called ersatz coffee. It was just like dark water. And we got a piece of bread. When we came to work, they gave us another piece of bread with some soup from vegetables, a piece of potato, no meat. And when we came home, we got another soup over there with some black coffee at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sleeping arrangements like?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned, the sleeping arrangements was we slept on wooden bridges with open straw thrown in. And that's the way we slept. We had a blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In one room could have been 200 or 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were their sanitary facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a sanitary facility, yes. It wasn't like here. But the farmers came and they cleaned out the sewer systems most of the time. We saw those wagons come and clean it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given special clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The clothing I brought with me, that's all I had. But when I got in touch with my father, I could got-- in the early days we could get some more clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family send you anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I got some more clothing, what-- winter clothing, pants, shoes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any type of cultural activity in the evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolute nothing. But except in the early days, they did perform some religious prayers. Again, it was a Jewish camp. And in those days it was before the Russian war started. And they let us be. And so they didn't mix in too much into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish people reported praying. Or they had a prayer in the evening. They put on the site. Most of them were not religious. Or if they were religious, they didn't care for it. They lost the faith. Were you able to follow the Jewish calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have a Jewish calendar, no. But the religious people had their signs, good or bad. They came up at certain dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there until February 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop now to change the tape.
